title: South Korea Formulates Sixth Basic Plan to Funnel 200 Trillion Won ($128.8 Billion USD) into Massive R&D Strategy through 2030 - Quantum Computing Report
gist: South Korea's Sixth Basic Plan (2026-2030) commits over 200 trillion won ($128.8B) to public R&D, earmarking 60 trillion won across 55 core technologies including AI, semiconductors, bio, and quantum, plus 260,000 GPUs and a sweeping research-bureaucracy overhaul to secure technological sovereignty.

insights.claim: South Korea's Ministry of Science and ICT committed over 200 trillion won ($128.8 billion) in public R&D over 2026-2030, earmarking 60 trillion won concentrated across 55 targeted technology classifications within ten strategic fields including AI, semiconductors, bio, and quantum computing.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Sizes a durable multi-year demand pool for quantum, AI, and semiconductor supply chains serving Korea; a macro tailwind for exposed vendors.
insights.quote: the government committed to investing over 200 trillion won ($128.8 billion USD) in public-funded research and development over a five-year period
insights.locator: The Ministry of Science and ICT has finalized

insights.claim: The plan marks a sharp shift from prior fiscal-discipline programs, introducing stable funding baselines explicitly to insulate local supply chains against volatile external macroeconomic disruptions.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A policy regime change toward guaranteed baseline funding reduces R&D cyclicality risk for Korean deep-tech; more predictable procurement.
insights.quote: marks a sharp shift from prior fiscal discipline programs, introducing stable funding baselines to insulate local supply chains
insights.locator: Managed under a unified national coordination system

insights.claim: South Korea will secure 260,000 public-private GPUs alongside a sixth national supercomputer and a National AI Computing Center, targeting an increase in the baseline AI service experience rate from 44.5% to over 70% by 2030 via an 'AI for Everyone' framework.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A 260,000-GPU sovereign procurement is a material demand signal for GPU suppliers and data-center infrastructure; time it against the 2027 Special Acts.
insights.quote: the government will secure a massive pool of 260,000 public-private graphics processing units (GPUs)
insights.locator: To establish a world-leading computing base

insights.claim: Legal enforcement will be stabilized via the Special Act on AI Data Centers in February 2027 and the National Research Data Act in June 2027, with sovereign foundation models targeted by 2027 and commercial 6G rollouts by 2030.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Concrete legislative and technical milestone dates give investors checkpoints to monitor policy execution and 6G/foundation-model timing.
insights.locator: Legal enforcement structures will be stabilized

insights.claim: Quantum technologies are a designated national strategic field with stabilized long-horizon deep-tech funding pathways across quantum computing, secure quantum communications, and quantum sensing.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Explicit quantum inclusion in sovereign strategy signals sustained state funding; positive for Korea-linked quantum firms.

insights.claim: The plan scales carbon-free energy including next-generation nuclear fusion, small modular reactors under a Special Act, and an ultra-high-voltage HVDC west-coast transmission line connecting Saemangeum and Seohwaseong by 2030.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: SMR and HVDC commitments create energy-infrastructure demand tied to powering AI/semiconductor clusters; watch for named build projects.

insights.claim: The plan cuts research administrative forms by 90% (from 2,171 down to 154 formats), abolishes strict project-based evaluation grade scales to reward failure-tolerant high-risk R&D, and funds 20 'national scientists' annually plus 10,000 graduate scholarships and 4,000 postdocs to combat brain drain.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Structural reforms to reduce bureaucracy and retain talent could raise Korean research productivity and competitiveness over the decade.
insights.quote: cutting research administrative forms by 90% (reducing them from 2,171 down to 154 specific formats)
insights.locator: The Sixth Basic Plan fundamentally overhauls
